noteGrp.note: The text has "Alexandria," but Halicarnassus is obviously meant, as in the heading of the chapter below.
The MS. has l, not , as L.
The word seems to have dropped out of the text.
Insert before . Without this the meaning will be, “there is inserted above,” which seems contradictory.
This seems to refer to the MS. from which the scribe was copying, as the prologue of Moro is not inserted at this place in our MS.
Mich. fol. 167 v, 168 r; Greg. p. 78.
Probably Timostratus (Wright, C. B. M. p. 559). might well fall out before .
The text has "Justinian," but clearly Justin must be meant. ^

. Brockelmann quoting this passage gives doubtfully "erexit"; but, as = was wrapped up ("involutus est," Brock.), I take it as above. In Smith's Thesaurus it is proposed to read , but this could only mean "beat" or "dashed."
The MS. seems to have , not , as L. prints. The word is a rare one, and generally means "to be shaken."
The MS. has , not , as L. prints. For the meaning of the word see p. 199, note 4.
Perhaps the "Simas" of Procopius (Bell. Pers. i. 13). is probably corrupt, the scribe having taken it for a common noun.
Read for ; cf. Proc. l.c.. The Herulians, whom the Greeks frequently called Ἕλουροι, are meant.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints. See bk. S, ch. 5 (p. 206, note 5).
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for , as proposed by Prof. Nöldeke [Zeitschr. Deutsch. Morgenländ. Gesellsch. vol. xxxiii. p. 159, note 1).
The native title of the tribal chief: Arm. bdeashkh. See Nöldeke, l.c. note 2.
This number cannot be right, as Martyropolis was N. of the Tigris, 240 stades from Amida and 100 from Attachæ (Proc. Bell. Pers. i. 21).
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.

. I can make nothing of this, and only guess at the meaning. The literal rendering is "a palace which he filled," and it is perhaps just possible that the idea of bribery is concealed under it; but more probably the text is corrupt.
Or October. It is not stated whether it was the 1st or 2nd Theshrin.
, from the root , "to swell." This form occurs only here, but the Shafʿel is found in 7. 3, (p. 205, l. 5, L.). See Smith's Thesaurus, col. 3795.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The rest of this chapter and the following chapter are contained in Cod. Rom.
Read for . Cod. Rom. . Cod. Brit, is indistinct; the seems to have been obliterated.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.The meaning of the reading of Cod. Brit. is "of God."
"Dion." fol. 182 v*; of. Jo. Mal. p. 477.
Mich. fol. 181 r; Greg. H. E. p. 211.
(so MS., not , as L.). Mich. has , "followed it," which points to a reading . All the remainder of this chapter is exceedingly obscure and probably corrupt.
, from Prov. x. 14; cf. bk. 8, ch. 4 (p. 243, l. 17, L.).
Perhaps something has fallen out before this sentence. Mich. has "but, after Julian had written that it was his opinion, and the holy man had answered him twice, and he would not obey."
I supply these words from Mich.

These letters are contained in Add. MS. 17,200 and in Cod. Syr. Vat. 140, from which last extracts are given by the Assemani (Bibl. Vat. MSS. Catal. vol. iii. p. 323 ff.). Both these give the Syriac translation of Paul of Callinicus. Our author's translation is independent.
Cyr. Ep. 45 (Migne, Patrol. Græc. vol. lxxvii. p. 236).
Cyr. de Rect. Fid. ad Theod. Imp. 22.
. Mich. has , and therefore probably read , "set down the whole matter in a sentence." Paul. Call. has , which determines the sense of .
I cannot find this in the Quod Beata Maria Sit Deipara.
Possibly something has dropped out here. See the quotation in ch. 13 (p. 238); and so Paul. Call. Mich., however, has the same as our text, so that no alteration should be made in it.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for . The MS. has no dot, either above or below the letter.
Text, "Theodosius." Paul of Callinicus, however, has "Athanasius,"' and so Mich.
Here begins a short extract in Cod. Rom.
Here the extract in Cod. Rom. ends.
Cyr. de Rect. Fid. ad Theod. Imp. 21.
Insert before . So Paul of Callinicus.
seems to be a denominative verb from (Hamilton). Paul. Call. .
Insert before . But the sentence seems to be corrupt.
Here begins an extract in Cod. Rom.
After insert , with Cod. Rom. and Paul. Call.; so Mich.
After insert , with Cod. Rom.; so Mich.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
After insert , with Cod. Rom.; so Mich.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. (Paul. Call. ); so Mich.
Jas. ii. 21-24. With Cod. Rom. insert before ; so Mich. Paul. Call. agrees with Cod. Brit., giving the meaning "which confirm faith."
Read for before , with Cod. Rom.
This must be supplied in order to make sense. Paul. Call, has , "for the one Abraham, when divided into periods." Mich., like our text, omits the verb.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for , with Cod. Rom.; so Mich.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. So Mich.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Cod. Rom. here inserts in the margin a sentence from bk. 10, ch. 9 (see p. 313), which Mai prints as if it were part of the text.
There is no before in Cod. Rom., as Mai prints.
After insert with Cod. Rom. , which has dropped out in Cod. Brit, through homoioteleuton.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. So Paul. Call. () and Mich.
Read for , with Cod. Rom. So Paul. Call, and Mich.
Here the extract in Cod. Rom. ends.
θεωρία, the inner or allegorical meaning of a book.
Ep. III. ad Sev. (Add. 17,200, fol. 9 r).
"was answered"(?), is unintelligible, unless we insert a negative. Mich. has , "was stolen," and therefore read . This reading I adopt. Both renderings, however, are very doubtful in grammar.
Add. 17,200, fol. 38 ff. (Latin translation in Mai Spicilegium Romanum, x. p. 169).

. This word apparently occurs only here and in 7. 7 (p. 217, l. 11, L.). It is not given in Payne Smith's Thesaurus, and Brockelmann, who refers only to p. 217, 11, renders it "cogitavit, spectavit"; but from this passage it seems clear that the meaning is as above.
MS. , not , as L. The dictionaries give this word as = λουδάριος, "gladiator" or "brigand," but here and in ch. 16 (pp. 257, 258) it seems clear that the meaning is as above (=λοίδορος(?)).
Read for . Mich. has , "expecting," which represents ; but I cannot see any meaning in it.
At Dr. Hamilton's suggestion I read for . Mich., however, has "because you (it)," and must therefore have read .
We require for before . Mich., however, also has .
After the MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints. Mich. has , "in your writings."
I do not know whence this is taken.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The words are accidentally repeated in L.'s text, but not in the MS.
Dion. Areop. de Div. Nom. i. 4.
This is not in the extant portions of the Sermo de Fide.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Jo. Eph. Frag. (Anecd. Syr. ii. p. 386).
This is not in the text.
According to the heading the petitioners were not monks but bishops. In Mich., however, they are called "bishops and monks."
seems to have dropped out before .
is crossed out in the MS., but a copula of some kind is required.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.

For we should perhaps read , "grains of wheat " (Hamilton). In this case we should probably read for and render "easy for them (the reapers) to separate the things that are winnowed . . . from the wheat."
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
This sentence is very awkward and obscure. The general sense I owe to Dr. Hamilton, and I arrive at it by omitting .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints. Logically we require another before .
Omit before . It is doubtful whether the letter is really in the MS.
For , "I am,'' we should perhaps read , or , "I was."
I owe the translation of this difficult passage to Dr. Hamilton. For . we should have expected .
Quoted shortly from the third letter of Severus to Julian (Add. MS. 17,200, fol. 17).
If is right, it would seem to be connected with , "to impel." Ct., however, p. 73, 1. 8, 21 (L.), where the verb seems to mean "to revile." I cannot find the source of the quotation.
For we must read either or .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The negative has fallen out of the text.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
We must either insert another or omit , rendering "the prince was not there."
The rest of this sentence is contained in Cod. Rom. Instead, however, of "and is rich and fertile," it has "and the chief city and capital of the country is called Carthage."
I.e. Arians—followers of the Synod of Ariminum.
Lit. “From the reading of Scriptures.” Possibly some words have fallen out.
If the singular is genuine, the Gothic commandant, or possibly the king, would seem to be meant; but perhaps we should read , “their chief men.”
536. The text is , which is a confusion between "Τρισκαιδέκατον'' and "τεσσαρεσκαιδέκατον." Read , which is the nearer to historical fact.
Jo. Eph. Fragm. (Anecd. Syr. ii. p. 386).
535-6. In chs. 15 and 16 the date is rightly given as the thirteenth year of the Indiction (534-5). "τρίς" () and "τέσσαρες" () are easily confused: see note 1 above.
What follows is contained in an abbreviated form in Add. MS. 12,154, fol. 151.
Mich. l.c.; Greg. H. E. p. 205 ff.
17,202 has , and 12,154 , while Mich. and Greg. have . Asylus of Rhesaina is mentioned in Elijah's life of John of Constantia (ed. Kleyn, p. 59), whence I follow the reading of 17,202.
After insert , with 12,154: similarly Mich. and Greg.
The MS. seems to have (βιβλία), not , as L. prints.
This sentence is not in 17,202, but, being in 12,154 (which, however, omits the portents following) and in Mich., it must be presumed to have formed part of the original text.

, a very awkward phrase, the rendering of which I owe to Dr. Hamilton. The usual meaning of is "to be anxious,'' or "to neglect"; but, as means "a storm," and Mich, has , we may fairly render as above.
Instead of the rest of this sentence 12,154 has , "and had been brought up with him," which is probably an error for , "and shared his opinions."
Here the extract in 12,154 breaks off owing to the loss of a leaf in the MS.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
This passage is repeated in a ninth cent. chronicle in Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 14,642, fol. 29 v, which, however, in place of has , "and he tore it with his teeth."
For I read but the sentence is extremely obscure. Mich. has , "bringing." Dr. Hamilton suggests , "strength."
Read for . With the MS. reading we must render "desertion."
This sentence is hopelessly corrupt in the text. I take the sense from Mich., who seems to have omitted , and read for , and for .
Logically the before should be omitted.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
To get this sense we should probably read for . Mich., however, has , "part," which points to a reading . With the MS. reading the meaning will be "mock and divide.''
Read for . The sentence is awkward, but the sense is clear from Mich. .
The reference seems to be to Isa. xl. 9.
Read for , as in the parallel passage in ch. 25 (p. 289).
MS. not , as L., but , changed into . Perhaps read .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for before , as in the parallel passage in ch. 25 (p. 290).
If "the womb" is the antecedent we must read for ; but possibly the antecedent is "the union." Cf. p. 290, note 4.
, "ran," here and in the parallel passage in ch. 25 seems to be used to represent συνέδραμον, though no such meaning is recognised in lexicons.
After this name the names of Theodoret and Andrew are accidentally repeated in the MS.
I do not know from what part of Basil's works this quotation is taken.
The MS. has before , which L. does not print.
There seems to have been another letter before , and I insert . So Mich.
The sentence, as it stands, is ungrammatical, but this is clearly the meaning; there is the same difficulty in Mich.

. Perhaps the original Syriac translator of this letter wrote , the chief see; but Mich. has the same as our text, and, as the divergences between him and our text in these letters show that he drew not from our author but from a common source, we must not emend.

What follows in Mich. is entirely different from our text. Moreover, not only is the transition at this point abrupt, but the sentence is of doubtful grammar, the personal being used with a feminine pronoun. Probably, therefore, a leaf has been lost; but, as what follows is not in Mich. and cannot easily be joined on to his text, I leave the text as it stands. A leaf ends at this point.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
This is an awkward construction, and we should perhaps insert a word, rendering "resembling us in our passions. "
Probably some words have dropped out in this sentence.
It would accord better with the following clause if instead of we read , "the passions and miracles."

Eccles. iii. 14. I owe this reference and the translation of the difficult sentence which follows to Dr. Hamilton. I can scarcely think that this sentence represents what Severus wrote, but, as Mich. has practically the same, any corruption must be older than our author. See p. 277, note 2.
Mich. omits "but," and in place of , "parables," has , "the Scripture," making the clause run, "as is said in the Scripture in the spirit of Scripture, saying."
The reference is probably to 1 Cor. xiv. 2, as Dr. Hamilton suggests.
A fragment of the letter not contained in our author is found in Add. MS. 12,155, fol. 110, and the whole intervening portion, including this fragment, is given in Mich.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Mich, has , "with the arms of the Spirit," and therefore probably read instead of . The former, no doubt, represents what Severus wrote.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has before , which L. does not print.
Severus must have written "their open attacks"; but, as Mich. also has the singular possessive, we must not emend. See p. 277, note 2.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Possibly we should read , "for us"; so Mich.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Mich, "to deceive you in any of our affairs." He read therefore for , for , and for , which probably represents what Theodosius wrote.
I read for . No point either above or below the letter is visible in the MS. Mich. .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for , and so it appears to be corrected in the MS.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has before , not , as L. prints. So Mich., though it cannot represent what Anthimus wrote.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Read for , as in the parallel passage in ch. 21 (p. 274).
Read for ; or, perhaps, rather omit , and take as the antecedent, as in ch. 21 (p. 274, note 4).
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Perhaps read , "your canonical letter"; so Mich.
What follows in our text is omitted by Mich.
Not in the letter given above (ch. 24), but in the letter of Theodosius to Severus upon his election to the see (Brit. Mus. Add. MS. 14,602, fol. 2-4).
Insert after , as necessitated by the plural following. So also in the letter to Severus (Add. MS. 14,602).
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The sentence, as it stands in the text, is unintelligible, and from a comparison with the letter to Severus in Add. 14,602 it is clear that the bracketed words have fallen out.
has perhaps crept in from below (Hamilton).
I do not know the source of this quotation.
